segments.paradiso-33-g35: Ancor is an archaic/poetic form of ancora meaning 'still' or 'again' and precedes the clitic pronoun ti. Ti is the unstressed second-person object pronoun 'you'. Priego here is the first-person singular verb 'I beg/entreat' (medieval spelling) — literally 'I entreat you.'
segments.paradiso-33-g36: Regina is a vocative addressing word 'queen' (direct address), set off by a comma; it names the addressee of the entreaty.
segments.paradiso-33-g37: The first che is a relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a clause modifying 'regina.' Puoi is second-person singular present of potere 'you can/are able.' Ciò is a demonstrative 'that/what' and the second che is the relative 'that/which' introducing the content clause. Tu is the stressed subject pronoun 'you.' Vuoli is an archaic spelling of volere 'you want' (not the verb vuolare); because the provided senses list gives meanings for 'vuolare' (fly/rush) which do not fit, the correct sense is not among the provided options.
segments.paradiso-33-g38: Che here introduces the desired result or request 'that ...'; conservi is the second-person singular present subjunctive (or hortatory subjunctive in older usage) of conservare, 'you preserve/keep.' Sani is a masculine plural adjective 'sound/whole,' agreeing with the (omitted) plural object name (affetti).
segments.paradiso-33-g39: Dopo is the preposition 'after.' Tanto is an adverb meaning 'so much/so great' modifying the verbal noun that follows. Veder is an infinitive used as a noun (verbal noun) 'seeing' or 'sight' — poetically 'after so great a sight/vision.'
segments.paradiso-33-g40: Li is the masculine plural definite article 'the' (used before affetti). Affetti is a masculine plural noun 'affections/feelings/passions' referring to the emotions in question. Suoi is the masculine plural possessive 'his/their' agreeing with affetti; it marks whose affections are meant.
segments.paradiso-33-g41: Vinca is the third-person singular present subjunctive (an optative/jussive use) of vincere, here meaning 'may ... conquer'. The verb comes before its subject (tua guardia), a common poetic inversion: 'May your guard conquer ...'. 'i movimenti umani' is the plural direct object ('the human movements/impulses'), with the definite article i and the adjective umani agreeing in number and gender.
segments.paradiso-33-g42: Vedi is an imperative (addressing Beatrice) meaning 'see' or 'look'. Beatrice is a vocative. 'con quanti beati' is an exclamatory prepositional phrase 'with how many blessed (ones)'. 'Per li miei prieghi' is an ablative/causal phrase 'through/on account of my prayers' (li is the archaic plural article). 'Ti chiudon le mani' has chiudon (poetic truncation of chiudono) = 3rd pl. present 'they close' or 'they fold'; ti is the indirect object 'to you', le mani 'the hands' (their hands). Word order places the verb before its object for metrical/emphatic effect: 'they close their hands to you.'
segments.paradiso-33-g43: This segment is punctuation: a full stop marking the end of the preceding exclamation. There are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.paradiso-33-g44: Li occhi is the definite plural 'the eyes' (Li is the older form of gli). 'da Dio' is an agent/source phrase 'by/from God' with da used passively/adjectivally. 'diletti e venerati' are past participles used adjectivally after the noun: 'beloved and venerated.' The whole phrase reads 'the eyes beloved and venerated by God.'
segments.paradiso-33-g45: Fissi is a past participle/adjectival form agreeing with occhi (masculine plural) meaning 'fixed' or 'fixedly gazing'. 'ne l’orator' (ne + l' = on the speaker) means 'upon the speaker' or 'on the speaker', so the clause says the eyes were fixed on the speaker. The adjective is predicative and refers back to 'Li occhi.'
segments.paradiso-33-g46: Ne here refers back to that fixation—'in/from that' or 'thereby'—and dimostraro is passato remoto 3rd plural 'they showed' (the eyes showed). Quanto introduces a content clause 'how much/how' and the clause quanto i devoti prieghi le son grati literally means 'how much the devout prayers are pleasing to them': i devoti prieghi is the subject, son (archaic for sono) is 'are', and le is the dative/indirect object referring to the eyes ('to them'), with grati 'pleasing.'
segments.paradiso-33-g47: Indi is an adverb 'then/thereupon'. 'a l’etterno lume' is a prepositional phrase 'to the eternal light' (a + l' = al). S’addrizzaro is the passato remoto 3rd plural of the pronominal verb addrizzarsi ('they turned/directing themselves'), with the reflexive pronoun s' attached; it means the eyes turned themselves toward that eternal light.
segments.paradiso-33-g48: Nel qual = 'in which' (nel = in + il, qual = which). Non si dee creder is an impersonal construction 'one ought not to believe' (dee = archaic 'deve', si impersonal). Che introduces the subordinate clause. S’invii is a subjunctive/pronominal form 'is sent' (si + invii, here impersonal/passive sense), with inverted order 's’invii per creatura l’occhio' meaning 'that the eye is sent through/by a creature'. 'Per creatura' = 'by/through any creature', and tanto chiaro modifies occhio = 'so bright/clear an eye.'
segments.paradiso-33-g145: This segment is a single period (full stop) printed on its own line; it functions as punctuation marking the end of a line or short printed element rather than carrying lexical content. There are no words to analyze grammatically.
segments.paradiso-33-g146: Like the previous segment, this is just a period (full stop) as printed punctuation, serving to mark an ending or separation on the page rather than expressing grammatical content.
segments.paradiso-33-g147: The leading '= ellipsis' is an editorial or typographic label indicating omitted material (here printed with an equals sign and the word 'ellipsis'). The remainder, 'End of Project Gutenberg's La Divina Commedia di Dante,' is a noun phrase announcing that this edition (owned/produced by 'Project Gutenberg') of the work titled in Italian is finished; note the possessive 'Project Gutenberg's' (’s) attaches to the edition, and the Italian title keeps its article and preposition 'La ... di Dante' intact. The trailing comma prepares for the following agent phrase.
segments.paradiso-33-g148: 'by Dante Alighieri' is a prepositional phrase identifying the author/agent of the work: the preposition 'by' marks authorship, followed by the author's full name in the normal English order (given name then family name). Grammatically it completes the preceding announcement by naming who wrote the work.
segments.paradiso-33-g1: A vocative address made of two nouns in apposition: Vergine ("Virgin") + Madre ("Mother"). In Italian the vocative uses the same forms as the nominative, so there is no copula here — it is simply calling her by these titles.
segments.paradiso-33-g2: A paradoxical appositive phrase: figlia ("daughter") + del (contraction of di + il, "of the") + tuo (possessive "your") + figlio ("son"). The preposition + article (del) marks the genitive; word order is regular (noun + prepositional phrase).
segments.paradiso-33-g3: Two contrasting adjectives joined by e ("and"): umile ("humble/lowly") and alta ("high/lofty"); then a comparative phrase più che ("more than / more than a") modifying creatura. The phrase means she is both humble and exalted beyond a mere creature.
segments.paradiso-33-g4: A noun phrase: termine fisso ("fixed term/limit/appointed end") followed by d’ (di elided) etterno consiglio ("of eternal counsel"). Etterno is an archaic spelling of eterno; d’ is the contracted preposition + article marking possession/agency.
segments.paradiso-33-g5: Tu se’ is the clausal subject and copula (tu + sei contracted to se’ = "you are"). Colei che introduces a relative clause ("she who/that"); l’umana natura is the direct object of the verb nobilitasti (passato remoto, 2nd person singular): "you ennobled human nature." Sì here intensifies the preceding verb ("so/indeed").
segments.paradiso-33-g6: The relative clause continues: che = "so that/that"; ’l suo fattore = "its (human nature’s) Maker" (l' = the; suo = its); non disdegnò (passato remoto, 3rd sg.) = "did not disdain"; di farsi sua fattura = infinitive construction with reflexive farsi ("to make himself / to become himself") + sua fattura ("his own creation/work"). The infinitive + suo fattura expresses that the Creator did not disdain to make himself the creature of that nature.
segments.paradiso-33-g7: Adverbial prepositional phrase Nel ventre tuo ("in your womb"); si + raccese forms a reflexive/impersonal construction (here passive-like: "love was rekindled"). Raccese is passato remoto (3rd sg. form of raccendere) with l’amore as grammatical subject.
segments.paradiso-33-g8: Per lo cui caldo = "by/through whose warmth" (per + article lo + cui as relative pronoun with genitive sense "whose"); ne l’etterna pace = "in the eternal peace" (ne = archaic/in poetic use meaning 'in' or 'within' + l' = the); così è germinato = present perfect/passive sense with essere as auxiliary ("thus has germinated/sprung forth"); questo fiore = the subject of germinato ("this flower").
segments.paradiso-33-g9: Qui ("here") + se’ ("you are"); a noi is a prepositional phrase meaning "to us" (a + noi). Meridïana face is a compound metaphor: meridiana (belonging to noon/meridian) modifies face ("torch" in medieval usage), so 'meridian torch' — an image of a sun-like guide of charity. di caritate = "of charity" (genitive).
segments.paradiso-33-g10: Simple conjunction and adverb: e = "and"; giuso = archaic/poetic form meaning "below" or "down" (used to introduce the next clause).
segments.paradiso-33-g11: Intra ’ mortali = "among/between mortals." Intra is a preposition here meaning 'among' (poetic/archaic); ’ mortali is the plural noun "mortals." The phrase sets the locative scope for the following clause.
segments.paradiso-33-g12: Se’ ("you are") introduces a second predicate; di speranza = "of hope" (genitive); fontana vivace = "a living/lively fountain" (noun + adjective). The whole means you are (also) a lively fountain of hope for mortals below.
segments.paradiso-33-g13: A single vocative noun: Donna ("Lady"/"Woman"). Here it is used respectfully as an address ("Lady").
segments.paradiso-33-g14: Se’ = "you are." Tanto ... e tanto = repeated intensifier "so... and so..." modifying grande ("great") and vali (present tense of valere, "you are worth/you have power/you avail"). The construction states her great status and efficacy.
segments.paradiso-33-g15: Che introduces a result/consequence clause: qual vuol grazia = "whoever wants/asks for grace" (qual used in the sense 'whoever/anyone who'); e a te non ricorre = "and does not resort/turn to you" (ricorre, present tense, 'turns to/resorts to' with the preposition a marking the object).
segments.paradiso-33-g16: Sua disïanza = "his/her longing/desire" (sua = possessive; disïanza here means desire/longing in context). Vuol volar = "wants to fly" (vuol = wants; volar = infinitive 'to fly'). Sanz’ (contraction of senza) ali = "without wings." The metaphor says that the person's desire attempts to fly but lacks the wings (i.e., lacks the means) because he did not turn to her.
segments.paradiso-33-g83: This is a vocative opening (Oh = O) followed by the noun phrase 'abbondante grazia' (abundant grace). 'ond’' is a contracted form of 'onde/ond' meaning 'by which/from which' and introduces a subordinate clause; 'io presunsi' is passato remoto (I presumed/dared) in the first person singular, and it governs the infinitive 'ficcar' (to thrust/fix), so the sequence is verb of daring + bare infinitive. 'Lo viso' is the direct object with the masculine article 'lo' (poetic usage before certain words), and the prepositional phrase 'per la luce etterna' indicates the goal/place (into/through the eternal light).
segments.paradiso-33-g84: 'Tanto che' is a result marker meaning 'so much that'; it introduces a consequential clause. Grammatically 'la veduta' (the sight/vision) is the direct object of the verb 'consunsi' (passato remoto, I consumed/wore away), with the clitic adverb 'vi' giving a locative sense 'there' (in that light). The verb is first person past and the construction states the result of the earlier daring.
segments.paradiso-33-g85: 'Nel suo profondo' is a locative phrase 'in its depth' (suo refers back to the eternal light). 'Vidi' is passato remoto 'I saw'; 'che' introduces the subordinate content clause. 'S’' is the reflexive clitic and 'interna' here functions as a present-state verb/adjective meaning 'is enclosed/contained within itself' (present descriptive after a past-seeing verb).
segments.paradiso-33-g86: 'Legato' is a past participle used adjectivally ('bound'); 'con amore' indicates manner ('with love'); 'in un volume' is a metaphorical locative ('into one volume/book'). The whole phrase modifies what was seen and is built like a reduced relative clause.
segments.paradiso-33-g87: 'Ciò che' = 'that which' introduces a relative clause; 'per l’universo' gives the scope ('throughout the universe'); the verb 'si squaderna' is reflexive present indicative meaning 'is spread out/exposed' (the reflexive voice describes how the items appear across the universe).
segments.paradiso-33-g88: This is a paratactic series of philosophical categories: 'sustanze' (substances) and 'accidenti' (accidents, in the scholastic sense) and 'lor costume' ('their custom' = their disposition/natural mode). 'Quasi conflati insieme' is a participial phrase, 'as if conflated/fused together' (quasi + past participle), describing how these elements are joined.
segments.paradiso-33-g89: 'Per tal modo che' ('in such a way that') introduces the consequence clause. 'Ciò ch’i’ dico' = 'that which I say' (relative clause with the enclitic ch’), and 'è un semplice lume' is a present indicative predication: 'is but a simple light' (a modest or inadequate description).
segments.paradiso-33-g90: The phrase is a main clause: 'La forma universal di questo nodo' is the noun phrase subject ('the universal form of this knot') followed by the verb 'credo' ('I believe'). 'ch’ i’ vidi' is a contracted subordinate clause (ch’ = che, i’ = io) meaning 'that I saw'; the old orthography shows elisions with apostrophes. Word order is subject + verb + subordinate clause.
segments.paradiso-33-g91: Here 'perché' = 'because' (not a question), introducing the reason clause. 'più di largo' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'more at large / at greater length' (più = more; di introduces the comparative sense with 'largo' used adverbially). The short word order reflects Dante's poetic economy.
segments.paradiso-33-g92: 'dicendo questo' is a gerund phrase ('saying this' / 'in saying this') functioning adverbially to qualify the preceding 'perché...' — it explains in what respect the speaker feels the reason; the gerund (dicendo) links the act of speaking to the speaker's feeling.
segments.paradiso-33-g93: 'mi sento' = 'I feel to me' (mi = dative/indirect object), a present-tense reflexive-like construction meaning 'I feel' or 'it seems to me'. The following 'ch’i’ godo' is another contracted subordinate clause (ch’ = che, i’ = io) meaning 'that I enjoy.' The sequence links the feeling ('mi sento') to its content introduced by 'che'.
segments.paradiso-33-g94: This is a comparative construction. 'Un punto solo' ('a single point/moment') is the subject; 'm’è' = 'mi è' literally 'is to me' (dative of interest) yields the sense 'seems to me' or 'is to me'; 'maggior letargo' is the predicate noun 'a greater lethargy'. 'che' introduces the comparison 'than ...' followed by the phrase 'venticinque secoli a la ’mpresa' ('twenty-five centuries to the enterprise') where 'a la ’mpresa' (elided 'impresa') means 'with respect to the undertaking'; the relative 'che' after it introduces 'fé' (archaic passato remoto of fare, 'made/caused') plus the infinitive 'ammirar' ('to marvel/admire') — an old infinitive form — so the clause means 'that made Neptune marvel at the shade of Argo.'
segments.paradiso-33-g17: La tua benignità is a noun phrase with definite article + possessive ("your kindness") serving as the subject. non pur soccorre is present indicative (soccorre, 3rd sg.) preceded by non pur, an idiom meaning "not only" (negation + pur gives the sense "not merely"). a chi domanda is an indirect-object phrase meaning "to whoever asks," with chi functioning as an indefinite relative/indefinite pronoun and domanda a present indicative "asks."
segments.paradiso-33-g18: ma introduces a contrast ("but"). molte fïate functions adverbially as "many times / often." liberamente is an adverb modifying precorre. al dimandar is the preposition a + definite article + infinitive used as a noun ("to/at the asking" or better "the asking/request"); precorre is present indicative (3rd sg.) of the verb precorrere meaning "go before / anticipate," so the clause says the kindness often freely anticipates the request.
segments.paradiso-33-g19: In te misericordia is an inverted, elliptical locative clause: literally "in you mercy," i.e. "mercy is in you." The copula (is) is omitted for rhetorical effect; misericordia is a feminine abstract noun placed after the locative prepositional phrase for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-33-g20: in te pietate repeats the same inverted locative construction: "pity (or piety) is in you." The noun pietate (pity/piety) is again placed after the prepositional phrase for rhetorical accumulation.
segments.paradiso-33-g21: in te magnificenza continues the triadic enumeration: "magnificence (or generosity) is in you." Same elliptical pattern: prepositional phrase + abstract noun, used for poetic emphasis.
segments.paradiso-33-g22: in te s’aduna is a pronominal verb construction: s’ (reflexive pronoun) + aduna (3rd sg.) = "gathers/comes together in you." Quantunque here means "whatever / all that," introducing the clause quantunque in creatura è di bontate = "whatever of goodness there is in any creature." The clause as a whole says that all goodness found in any created thing gathers in you.
segments.paradiso-33-g23: Or is a discourse particle meaning "now"; questi is a demonstrative pronoun "this one" (used as the subject). The two together introduce the person who will be described by the following relative clause.
segments.paradiso-33-g24: This is a relative clause introduced by che ("who/that"). da l’infima lacuna de l’universo infin qui is a prepositional phrase meaning "from the lowest hollow/gap of the universe up to here." ha vedute is the perfect construction (avere + past participle) meaning "has seen;" vedute is plural to agree with le vite that follows ("the spiritual lives"). ad una ad una is a repetitive expression meaning "one by one." The whole clause modifies questi: "who has seen the spiritual lives, one by one, from the universe’s lowest hollow up to here."
segments.paradiso-33-g25: supplica is present indicative (3rd sg.) meaning "he begs/pleads" and takes an indirect object introduced by a: a te = "to you." The verb expresses earnest entreaty directed at the addressee.
segments.paradiso-33-g26: per grazia is an adverbial prepositional phrase: per = "by/through/as" and grazia = "grace/favor," here indicating that the petition is made "by/through grace" or "as a favor."
segments.paradiso-33-g27: di virtute tanto is a measure phrase: di = "of," virtute = "virtue / power / efficacy," and tanto = "so much / so great." This quantifies the gift being requested and links to the result clause introduced by che that follows.
segments.paradiso-33-g28: che introduces a result/purpose clause: possa is present subjunctive (3rd sg.) of potere, expressing possibility/wish ("may be able"). con li occhi is instrumental "with the eyes" (li is the archaic masculine plural article used here); levarsi is a pronominal infinitive "to lift oneself up." più alto is a comparative/adverbial phrase "higher," verso l’ultima salute = "toward the ultimate salvation." Grammatically, the subjunctive marks the hoped-for ability: "that he may, with his eyes, lift himself higher toward final salvation."
segments.paradiso-33-g29: E io is simply the conjunction + pronoun "and I," marking the speaker as the subject who follows with his own plea.
segments.paradiso-33-g30: This relative clause (che ...) modifies io. mai ... non arsi is a negative past expression: mai (= "ever") with non yields a sense of "never"; arsi is the past form of the verb "to burn/be inflamed" used metaphorically. piu ch’i’ fo per lo suo contains a comparative: più ... che "more than" and fo (1st sg. of fare, archaic) "I do / I feel" — so the clause says he has never been so inflamed for his own sight as I am for his. The construction mixes perfective wording with a present-tense comparison (fo = I do), a typical poetic usage.
segments.paradiso-33-g31: tutti miei prieghi ti porgo is straightforward: tutti miei prieghi ("all my petitions/prayers") is the direct object; ti is the indirect object "to you;" porgo is 1st person present of porgere, "I present/offer." The speaker is formally offering all his petitions to the addressee.
segments.paradiso-33-g32: e priego che non sieno scarsi: priego (1st sg. present) "I pray/entreat" introduces a subordinate clause with che + present subjunctive sieno (3rd pl. of essere) expressing the wish that the petitions "not be scarce/few." scarsi here means "few / scant," so the speaker asks that his prayers not be insufficient.
segments.paradiso-33-g33: perché here functions as a purpose/result marker ("so that / so as to") and introduces a clause with the subjunctive disleghi (2nd sg. present subjunctive) addressed to tu: "that you may unbind every cloud from his mortality with your prayers." ogne nube = "every cloud/veil," di sua mortalità = "from his mortality," co’ prieghi tuoi = "with your prayers." The verb disleghi is subjunctive because it expresses the hoped-for action.
segments.paradiso-33-g34: sì che introduces a result clause: the first sì is "so/thus" and che "that." 'l sommo piacer = "the supreme pleasure/delight." li si dispieghi combines the dative pronoun (li, here "to him" in older usage) with the reflexive si and dispieghi (present subjunctive 3rd sg. of dispiegare) to mean "may it be unfolded to him." The subjunctive expresses the wished-for result: that the supreme delight be revealed to him.
segments.paradiso-33-g49: E io ch’al fine di tutt’ i disii appropinquava: 'ch’' is the contracted relative 'che' meaning 'who'; the clause modifies 'io' and the main verb is 'appropinquava' (imperfect indicative) meaning 'was approaching' or 'drew near'. 'Al fine' = 'to the end' (a + il), and 'tutt’ i disii' is an archaic elision of 'tutti i desideri' ('all the desires'). The word order places the relative clause between subject and verb, a typical poetic insertion.
segments.paradiso-33-g50: sì com’ io dovea: 'sì com’' is a contracted form of 'sì come' = 'as' or 'just as'; 'dovea' is the imperfect of 'dovere' used to mean 'I was supposed to / I ought to' (a polite or required action in the past). The whole phrase is a parenthetical qualification ('as I ought').
segments.paradiso-33-g51: l’ardor del desiderio in me finii: 'l’ardor del desiderio' = 'the ardor/passion of desire'; 'in me' = 'within me'; 'finii' is the first-person passato remoto (simple past) of 'finire' (here 'I ended' or 'I brought to an end'), with the subject 'io' from the previous clause. The verb places the completed action at a point in the narrative.
segments.paradiso-33-g52: Bernardo m’accennava: 'm’' is the clitic for the indirect object ('to me'), and 'accennava' is the imperfect of 'accennare' meaning 'he made a sign/beckoned' or 'gestured'. The imperfect gives a descriptive, ongoing action in the past.
segments.paradiso-33-g53: e sorridea: 'sorridea' is the imperfect of 'sorridere' meaning 'he smiled'. It coordinates with the previous imperfect action (he gestured and he smiled), giving a simultaneous descriptive image.
segments.paradiso-33-g54: perch’ io guardassi suso: here 'perch’' (short for 'perché') introduces a purpose clause meaning 'so that'; it is followed by the imperfect subjunctive 'guardassi' (subjunctive used for purpose after a past-tense main verb), meaning 'that I might look' or 'so that I would look' and 'suso' = 'upward'.
segments.paradiso-33-g55: ma io era già per me stesso tal qual ei volea: 'era' is the imperfect of 'essere' ('I was'), 'già' = 'already'. 'Per me stesso' means 'in myself' or 'by myself' (i.e. inwardly); 'tal qual' = 'such as' and 'ei volea' ('volea' imperfect of 'volere') = 'he wished/desired'. The clause asserts that Dante already had the inner condition Bernard wanted.
segments.paradiso-33-g56: ché la mia vista,: 'ché' = 'for'/'because', introducing the reason. 'La mia vista' = 'my sight' (subject of the coming clause) — short nominative phrase preparing the explanation.
segments.paradiso-33-g57: venendo sincera,: 'venendo' is the gerund of 'venire' used adverbially ('coming' or 'as it came'); here it describes the manner of the sight. 'Sincera' means 'pure' or 'untainted' and modifies 'vista'. This gerund phrase qualifies how the sight behaved or presented itself.
segments.paradiso-33-g58: e più e più intrava per lo raggio de l’alta luce che da sé è vera.: 'e più e più' = 'more and more' (repetition for emphasis); 'intrava' is the imperfect of 'intrare' used transitively/intransitively as 'penetrate/enter' ('penetrated'); 'per lo raggio' = 'through the ray' and 'de l’alta luce' = 'of the lofty/high light'. The relative 'che da sé è vera' means 'which is true by itself' (self-evident or self-originating).
segments.paradiso-33-g59: Da quinci innanzi il mio veder fu maggio che ’l parlar mostra,: 'Da quinci innanzi' is a set phrase meaning 'from that point onward' or 'thenceforward'. 'Il mio veder' (my seeing/sight) is the subject; 'fu' is past 'was'. 'Maggio' is an archaic adjective meaning 'greater' (compare moderno 'maggiore') and the comparative is introduced by 'che' here meaning 'than', so the clause says 'my sight was greater than speech can show'.
segments.paradiso-33-g60: ch’a tal vista cede,: 'ch’' (che) = 'which'; 'a tal vista' = 'to such a sight/vision'; 'cede' (present indicative of 'cedere') = 'yields' or 'gives way'. The clause describes speech's inability to match that vision.
segments.paradiso-33-g61: e cede la memoria a tanto oltraggio.: 'e cede' = 'and yields' (again present indicative), 'la memoria' = 'memory', 'a tanto oltraggio' = 'to such an outrage' or 'to so great an affront' (here 'oltraggio' = the overpowering effect of the vision). The phrase means memory, too, gives way under the overwhelming experience.
segments.paradiso-33-g62: Qual è colüi che sognando vede,: 'Qual è colui' is an illustrative comparison 'as is the one' or 'such is he who'; 'che sognando vede' is a relative clause 'who, dreaming, sees' (with 'sognando' as a gerund/adverbial participle 'while dreaming' and 'vede' present indicative 'sees'). The whole line sets up an analogy.
segments.paradiso-33-g63: che dopo ’l sogno la passione impressa rimane,: 'che' continues the relative idea; 'dopo il sogno' = 'after the dream'; 'la passione impressa' = 'the impression/emotion imprinted' (passione = strong feeling, 'impressa' = impressed/engraved in the mind); 'rimane' present = 'remains' or 'lingers'.
segments.paradiso-33-g64: e l’altro a la mente non riede,: 'e l’altro' = 'and the other' (i.e. the rest/details of the dream); 'a la mente' = 'to the mind'; 'non riede' uses 'riedere' (present) meaning 'does not return'. The clause contrasts the lingering feeling with the loss of the rest of the content.
segments.paradiso-33-g65: cotal son io,: simple copular statement 'such am I' with the inverted poetic order (adjective before verb). 'Cotal' = 'such', 'son' = 'am' (poetic present of 'essere').
segments.paradiso-33-g66: ché quasi tutta cessa mia visïone,: 'ché' = 'for/because'; 'quasi tutta' = 'almost entirely'; 'cessa' (present) = 'ceases' or 'gives way'; 'mia visïone' = 'my vision/visual experience'. The clause explains why he is like the dreaming man: most of the vision has already ceased.
segments.paradiso-33-g67: e ancor mi distilla nel core il dolce che nacque da essa.: 'e ancor' = 'and still'; 'mi' is the indirect object clitic meaning 'to me'; 'distilla' (present of 'distillare') is used metaphorically 'distils' or 'drips' into the heart; 'nel core' = 'into the heart'; 'il dolce' = 'the sweetness' (noun here); 'che nacque da essa' = 'that was born from it' (i.e. born from that vision). The clause says that although the vision has mostly ceased, its sweetness continues to trickle into him.
segments.paradiso-33-g95: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' or 'so' and sets the manner of what follows. 'La mente mia' is a normal noun phrase with the definite article + noun + postposed possessive ('mia' after 'mente' is common in poetry), meaning 'my mind.'
segments.paradiso-33-g96: Tutta here modifies 'mente' and is used adverbially/qualificationally as 'wholly' or 'completely.' 'Sospesa' is a past participle used adjectivally to mean 'suspended' or 'hanging', describing the state of the mind.
segments.paradiso-33-g97: Mirava is the imperfect indicative of mirare, 'was gazing' (continuous past action). 'Fissa' is an adjective meaning 'fixed' or 'steady', here specifying how the mind gazed (fixedly / intent).
segments.paradiso-33-g98: Immobile 'motionless' and attenta 'attentive' are two coordinating adjectives describing the gaze; immobile is invariable and attenta agrees with mente (feminine). The conjunction e simply links the two adjectives.
segments.paradiso-33-g99: This clause uses an older word-order: 'di mirar' (= 'with regard to looking' / 'to look') + faceasi, an enclitic form corresponding to si facea (imperfect of fare with reflexive si) meaning 'became/was making itself'; accesa is a past participle 'kindled' (figuratively 'inflamed' with desire). So: 'and was always kindled with (a desire) to look.'
segments.paradiso-33-g100: A (here 'at' or 'before') + quella luce 'that light' establishes the reference; cotal is a poetic/archaic form of cotale meaning 'such' or 'of such a sort.' 'Si diventa' is the impersonal/reflexive construction with diventare in the present indicative: 'one becomes / one is made (such).' The clause states a resulting condition.
segments.paradiso-33-g101: This relative/subordinate clause begins with che 'that.' Volgersi is a reflexive infinitive 'to turn oneself' (here 'to turn away'); da lei = 'from it/her.' Per altro aspetto means 'to another aspect/appearance' (per = 'for/in regard to' or 'to' here). È impossibil is an archaic short form meaning 'it is impossible'; che mai si consenta is a following clause using the subjunctive consenta (present subjunctive) with the impersonal si: 'that ever one consent / that it ever be allowed.'
segments.paradiso-33-g102: Però che introduces the explanatory clause 'because' / 'for'; here therefore/because. ’l is the elided form of il (the), and ben is the abstract noun 'the good' (the moral/philosophical good) rather than an adverb.
segments.paradiso-33-g103: Ch’è = che è 'which is'; del volere obietto = 'the object (obietto) of the will' (del = di + il). Volere is a substantive here ('will' as object of desire); obietto is the noun 'object' (what the will aims at). The phrase identifies what 'the good' is in philosophical terms.
segments.paradiso-33-g104: Tutto 'all' / 'entirely' + s’accoglie (si accoglie) uses the reflexive/passive si to mean 'is received/embraced' or 'embraces itself' in lei ('in it' / 'in that light'). The clause says the good is wholly taken up in that light.
segments.paradiso-33-g105: E 'and' links the consequence. Fuor di quella = 'outside of that.' È defettivo 'is defective' uses a predicate adjective; ciò ch’è lì perfetto is a relative clause 'what is perfect there.' Word order places the contrast ('what is perfect there') after the copula: outside that, the thing that is perfect there is defective.
segments.paradiso-33-g106: Omai 'henceforth' / 'now' opens the sentence; sarà is the future of essere 'will be.' Più corta mia favella is a comparative construction where più corta modifies favella ('my speech will be shorter'). The possessive mia follows the noun (poetic placement).
segments.paradiso-33-g107: Pur here means roughly 'even' or 'as far as' in the phrase 'pur a quel ch’io ricordo' meaning 'according to what I remember' / 'so far as I recall.' A quel che io ricordo is a prepositional phrase 'to/according to that which I remember.' Ricordo is present tense 'I remember / I recall.'
segments.paradiso-33-g108: Che here introduces the comparison 'than' (che + comparative = 'than'); d’un is di + un 'of a.' Fante in medieval usage can mean 'child' or 'infant' (here 'an infant'); the second che introduces a relative clause 'who wets' (bagni) — bagni is present subj/indicative form of bagnare used here as a finite verb 'wets/moistens' — ancor = 'still,' and la lingua a la mammella = 'his tongue at the breast' (i.e. nursing).
segments.paradiso-33-g109: Non perché 'not that' introduces a negative explanation. Più ch’un = 'more than one.' Semplice sembiante = 'a simple appearance' (semplice here = single/onefold or plain). Fosse is the imperfect subjunctive of essere ('there were' / 'there was') used in this concessive/explanatory construction. Nel vivo lume = 'in the living/vivid light' and ch’io mirava = 'that I was gazing at' (relative clause).
segments.paradiso-33-g110: Che here resumes/explains ('for'/'because'); tal è sempre qual s’era davante = 'it is always such as it was before.' Tal = such; s’era ('si era') is the imperfect 'it was' and davante means 'in front / before' (i.e. as it was earlier). This parenthetical confirms the appearance itself isn't changing externally.
segments.paradiso-33-g111: Ma per la vista 'but because of the sight/vision' introduces the real cause. Che s’avvalorava is a relative clause: s’avvalorava = si avvalorava (imperfect) from avvalorare meaning 'was becoming stronger / was being strengthened.' In me guardando places the effect 'in me' and the gerund guardando ('by looking') indicates the means by which the sight strengthened.
segments.paradiso-33-g112: Una sola parvenza = 'a single appearance/semblance.' Sola emphasizes 'single/one' more than merely 'alone.' Parvenza is an appearance or seeming aspect.
segments.paradiso-33-g113: Mutandom’ io is an inversion common in poetry: mutandom' (short for mutandomi) is the gerund 'changing (myself)' with the enclitic pronoun -mi attached; the subject io follows for emphasis: 'as I changed.' This indicates that the subject was undergoing change while the next clause happens.
segments.paradiso-33-g114: A me 'to me' + si travagliava uses si with the imperfect of travagliar(e) to express that something 'laboured on' or 'tormented' the speaker: literally 'it laboured upon me' / 'it troubled me.' The construction is impersonal/reflexive; travagliava is imperfect indicative.
segments.paradiso-33-g68: Così is an adverb introducing the simile (‘thus’). La neve is the definite noun phrase subject. Al = a + il gives a directional/agent phrase ‘to/by the sun’ (sol is a poetic form for sole/sun). Si disigilla is a middle/reflexive construction: disigilla is 3rd person singular present indicative of disigillare, with si making it ‘unseals itself / is unsealed’ (a present-tense general or timeless statement in a poetic simile).
segments.paradiso-33-g69: Così repeats the linking adverb. Al vento is a prepositional phrase indicating medium or agency (‘by/with the wind’). Ne le foglie is an older locative phrasing (ne = in, followed by the article le) meaning ‘in the leaves’; levi is an adjective agreeing with foglie (‘light, slight’). Si perdea is the imperfect 3rd-person singular with si (perdere intransitively/reflexively) meaning ‘was lost / kept being lost’ (imperfect marks ongoing or habitual past). La sentenza di Sibilla is the noun phrase the verb applies to (the Sibyl’s sentenza = utterance / saying / opinion).
segments.paradiso-33-g70: O somma luce is a vocative address (‘O highest Light’). Che is a relative pronoun referring back to luce. Tanto modifies the verb that follows; ti is the 2nd-person reflexive pronoun, and levi is 2nd-person singular present indicative of levare — ‘you lift yourself / withdraw yourself’ (Dante addresses the Light as ‘you’). Da’ is a contracted form of da + (article) or da + vowel elision and introduces the source: concetti mortali = ‘mortal conceptions / human thoughts.’
segments.paradiso-33-g71: A la mia mente is an indirect-object phrase ‘to my mind/memory’. Ripresta is a present tense verb (3rd singular) meaning ‘takes back / restores’ (an older form of riprendere/riprestare). Un poco is the quantity ‘a little’. Di quel che parevi is a genitive/partitive phrase + relative clause: quel = ‘that’, che introduces the relative clause; parevi is the imperfect (2nd-person singular in form) used here to report the Light’s appearance in the past — ‘what you seemed (to be).’
segments.paradiso-33-g72: E introduces an additional request (‘and’). Fa is 3rd-person singular present of fare used causatively here (‘make’). La lingua mia is the direct object (‘my tongue’), and tanto possente is a degree phrase + adjective: tanto modifies possente (‘so powerful’). The Light is the implied subject making the speaker’s tongue powerful.
segments.paradiso-33-g73: Ch’ is the contracted che introducing a final or result clause (‘that’). Una favilla sol = a single spark (favilla = spark; sol = poetic ‘only/alone’). De (an archaic form of di) la tua gloria = ‘of your glory’. Possa is the present subjunctive of potere (3rd sg) used to express a wish or potential result (‘may be able’ / ‘may’). Lasciare is the infinitive complement (‘to leave’), and a la futura gente marks the beneficiary/recipient (‘for future people’ / future generations).
segments.paradiso-33-g74: Ché is a conjunction meaning ‘for/because’ and here introduces the reason or explanation that follows.
segments.paradiso-33-g75: Per introduces purpose (‘in order to’). Tornare and sonare are infinitives expressing two parallel purposes: tornare alquanto a mia memoria = ‘to come back somewhat to my memory’ (alquanto = somewhat), and per sonare un poco in questi versi = ‘and to sound/play a little in these verses’ (sonare = to sound / to come through in the verse). In questi versi locates the second purpose ‘in these lines.’
segments.paradiso-33-g76: Più is an adverb ‘more’. Si conceperà is a 3rd-person singular future (conceperà) with si forming an impersonal/passive sense: ‘more will be conceived / more will be understood.’ Di tua vittoria is the genitive phrase ‘of your victory’ and completes the impersonal construction.
segments.paradiso-33-g77: Io is the emphatic subject pronoun ‘I’. Credo is 1st-person singular present indicative of credere: ‘I believe / I think’ — a self-contained main clause stating the speaker’s conviction.
segments.paradiso-33-g78: Per introduces the cause (‘because of’). L’acume = the sharpness or keenness (mental or of perception). Ch’io soffersi is a relative clause: ch’ = che ‘that’, io subject, soffersi is an older past form meaning ‘I suffered / experienced’ (a preterite/past tense). Del vivo raggio is a contracted di + il + noun phrase ‘of the living/vivid ray’ (the beam of light) supplying the source of that sharpness.
segments.paradiso-33-g79: Ch’ introduces the content clause resumed from the previous thought. I’ (i) is an abbreviated io (I). Sarei is the 1st-person singular conditional of essere (‘I would be’). Smarrito is a past participle used as predicate adjective meaning ‘lost / gone astray’ — together, ‘I would have been lost’ or ‘I would be lost.’
segments.paradiso-33-g80: Se introduces the conditional protasis (‘if’). Li occhi miei = the eyes of mine (li is the old article ‘the’ before occhi). Da lui marks direction/agent ‘from him / away from him’. Fossero is the imperfect subjunctive 3rd-person plural of essere (used in the protasis of a conditional). Aversi is an archaic/pronominal verb-form meaning ‘to turn away / to be averse’ used here with essere (fossero aversi = ‘had been turned away’); the whole clause expresses the contrary-to-fact condition on which the main clause’s result depends.
segments.paradiso-33-g81: E’ is an older or contracted form used here as a subject pronoun (contextually ‘it’ or impersonal subject) that introduces the clause; mi is the indirect object ‘to me’. Ricorda is 3rd-person singular present indicative ‘reminds’ or ‘tells’. Ch’io fui più ardito = ‘that I was bolder’ (ch’ = che ‘that’, io subject, fui = passato remoto or perfect of essere ‘I was’, più ardito = more daring). Per questo a sostener indicates purpose/reason (‘for this, in order to sustain/endure/support’): per + noun + a + infinitive is a common older sequence.
segments.paradiso-33-g82: Tanto introduces result or extent (‘so much’). Ch’i’ giunsi = 'so that I reached' (tanto ... ch’ = so ... that; i = abbreviated io, giunsi is passato remoto/preterite of giungere ‘I reached’). L’aspetto mio = ‘my look/visage/expression’ or here ‘my look (gaze)’. Col (con + il) valore infinito = ‘with infinite valor/power’ — the preposition col links giunsi to the manner/means: ‘I reached my gaze with infinite strength.’
segments.paradiso-33-g126: Quella circulazion is a demonstrative noun phrase ('that circular motion'). The relative clause che sì concetta pareva... modifies circulazion: che = 'which'; sì concetta is an adjectival past participle phrase ('thus conceived') with the adverb sì before the participle. Pareva is imperfect indicative, 3rd sing., agreeing with circulazion and meaning 'seemed'. In te is a prepositional phrase 'in you', and come lume reflesso is a simile 'like (a) reflected light' completing the predicate.
segments.paradiso-33-g127: da li occhi miei alquanto circunspetta is a parenthetical locution modifying the perception in the previous clause: da li occhi miei functions adverbially ('to/for my eyes' i.e. in the judgement of my eyes), alquanto = 'somewhat', and circunspetta (an adjective) = 'circumspect/restrained' describing how the sight appeared to the poet.
segments.paradiso-33-g128: dentro da sé is a compact locative/reflective phrase: dentro = 'within', da sé = 'of/from itself' (i.e. 'within itself' or 'by itself'), the preposition da + reflexive pronoun sé yields the idea that the thing is self-contained.
segments.paradiso-33-g129: del suo colore stesso: del = di + il, marking possession ('of the'); suo = 'its/their'; colore = 'colour/appearance'; stesso is an intensifier meaning 'same' or 'very' ('in its very colour'). Together this phrase specifies the circle's own colour.
segments.paradiso-33-g130: mi parve pinta de la nostra effige is a standard experiencer construction: mi = 'to me' (dative of experiencer), parve = passato remoto 'seemed', pinta (past participle used adjectivally) = 'painted', de (archaic for di) = 'with/of', la nostra effige = 'our effigy/likeness'. So: 'it seemed to me painted with our likeness.'
segments.paradiso-33-g131: per che ’l mio viso in lei tutto era messo is a result/clause introduced by per che ('wherefore/so that'). ’l mio viso = 'my face'; in lei refers back to the feminine circulazion ('in it'); tutto modifies 'era messo' ('was wholly placed'). Era messo is a passive/periphrastic construction ('was put/was set').
segments.paradiso-33-g132: Qual è ’l geomètra che tutto s’affige per misurar lo cerchio is a simile introduced by Qual è ('as is'/'such as is'): Qual è = 'as is/what is', ’l geomètra = 'the geometer'. Che introduces a relative clause. tutto s’affige is pronominal (si affigge) meaning 'applies himself wholly' (tutto = 'entirely'). Per misurar is purpose ('in order to measure'), lo cerchio = 'the circle'. Note s’affige is reflexive present indicative.
segments.paradiso-33-g133: e non ritrova is a simple continuation: e = 'and', non = negation, ritrova is present indicative 'finds/comes upon' (here 'does not find').
segments.paradiso-33-g134: pensando is a gerund ('thinking') that modifies the verb ritrova — 'cannot find, thinking' — indicating the geometer's mental activity while he searches.
segments.paradiso-33-g135: quel principio ond’ elli indige: quel principio = 'that principle'; ond' (ond' = onde/da cui) = 'from/whence/which' (i.e. 'on which'); elli is an archaic 3rd-person pronoun ('he'); indige is present indicative 'needs' or 'lacks'. The clause means 'that principle which he lacks/needs.'
segments.paradiso-33-g136: tal era io a quella vista nova: tal = 'such', era = imperfect 'I was', io = 'I', a quella vista nova = 'with/regarding that new sight/vision' — a literary dative/locative use of a indicating relation to the vision.
segments.paradiso-33-g137: veder voleva come si convenne l’imago al cerchio e come vi s’indova: volere in the imperfect + infinitive (veder voleva) expresses desire/attempt ('I wanted to see'). Come si convenne = 'how it was fitting' (si yields impersonal/passive sense). L’imago al cerchio = 'the image to/for the circle'; come vi s’indova = 'and how it is set/placed there' (s'indova is a pronominal verb meaning to fall into place / be set there).
segments.paradiso-33-g138: ma non eran da ciò le proprie penne: ma non = 'but not'; eran = imperfect 'were'; da ciò = 'for that' (da = 'for' here), le proprie penne = literally 'the proper feathers' — a metaphor for the poet's own powers/skills. The line means 'but my own powers were not sufficient for that.'
segments.paradiso-33-g139: se non che la mia mente fu percossa... is an idiomatic concessive phrase: se non che = 'except that/only that'; la mia mente fu percossa = 'my mind was struck' (fu + past participle percossa = perfect passive, 'was struck'); da un fulgore = 'by a brightness/flash'; in che sua voglia venne = 'in which its desire/will came' — 'che' = 'in which'. The clause explains the exception that allowed perception despite inadequate 'feathers'.
segments.paradiso-33-g140: A l’alta fantasia qui mancò possa: A l'alta fantasia is a dative-like construction ('to the lofty imagination' or 'for lofty imagination'), qui = 'here' (at this point), mancò = passato remoto/preterite 'lacked/fell short', and possa is used substantively as 'power/ability' (possa from potere). The line says that imaginative power failed at this point.
segments.paradiso-33-g141: ma già volgeva il mio disio e ’l velle: ma già = 'but already'; volgeva (imperfect) = 'was turning/was being directed'; il mio disio = 'my desire'; e ’l velle = 'and the will' (velle is an archaic noun for 'will' here). The two inner motions (desire and will) are personified as turning.
segments.paradiso-33-g142: sì come rota ch’igualmente è mossa is a simile: sì come = 'just as', rota = 'a wheel', ch' = 'that/which', igualmente = 'equally', è mossa = 'is moved' (passive participle mossa agrees with rota). The wheel metaphor describes uniform circular motion.
segments.paradiso-33-g143: l’amor che move il sole e l’altre stelle: l'amor = 'the Love' (often theological/powerful Love in Dante); che = relative 'which'; move = present indicative 'moves' or 'sets in motion'; il sole e l’altre stelle = 'the sun and the other stars'. The line names the ultimate mover as Love.
segments.paradiso-33-g144: This block is an editorial/table heading listing special typographic characters; it is not part of Dante's verse. The segment is a direct list with headings and equivalences (e.g. 'à = a grave'), so grammatically it is a sequence of labels and definitions rather than syntactic sentences.
segments.paradiso-33-g115: Ne here is the archaic/pre-poetic preposition meaning "in" and governs the following noun phrase. The noun phrase 'la profonda e chiara sussistenza de l’alto lume' is a genitive construction: 'sussistenza' (the subsistence/being) qualified by two adjectives ('profonda' 'deep/profound' and 'chiara' 'clear/bright') and then specified by the prepositional genitive 'de l’alto lume' (of the high/lofty light). Parvermi is an archaic past form of parere taking the experiencer as enclitic (mi): literally 'they appeared to me' (verb agrees with the following plural subject 'tre giri'), and the remainder 'tre giri di tre colori e d’una contenenza' names that subject and its attributes ('of three colours and of one/identical containment/circumference').
segments.paradiso-33-g116: 'E l’un da l’altro' is a coordinated phrase meaning 'and one from the other' (l’un = one, da l’altro = from the other). 'Come iri da iri' is a simile: come = 'like/as', and iri here is a poetic/archaic word (Dante's image of rainbow/iris); the repetition 'iri da iri' stresses mutual reflection. Parea is an imperfect/past of parere ('seemed/appeared'), and reflesso is a past participle functioning as predicate adjective 'reflected.'
segments.paradiso-33-g117: The clause begins with 'e' linking the idea: 'and the third seemed fire.' '’l terzo parea foco' = 'the third appeared (to me) to be fire.' The relative clause introduced by 'che' modifies 'foco': 'che quinci e quindi igualmente si spiri' — 'which is breathed/emanates equally from here and from there.' 'Si spiri' is reflexive/passive in form (si + spiri) and in Dante is a present subjunctive or jussive form used to describe an inherent/characteristic action (here: to be breathed/emanate); 'quinci' and 'quindi' mark the two opposite directions ('from this side' and 'from that side') and 'igualmente' qualifies the equality of the emission.
segments.paradiso-33-g118: This exclamation uses 'Oh quanto' = 'O how' to quantify the following predicate. 'È corto il dire' literally 'the saying/speech is short' — Dante uses 'corto' to mean insufficient/inadequate for the subject. The second coordinate 'e come fioco al mio concetto' uses 'come' = 'how' and 'fioco' = 'dim/weak' with the dative phrase 'al mio concetto' ('to/for my conception'), i.e. 'how faint/weak it is in relation to my apprehension.'
segments.paradiso-33-g119: A short connector: 'e questo' = 'and this' — 'questo' picks up the previously described vision. The comma marks a pause before the comparative phrase that follows.
segments.paradiso-33-g120: 'A quel ch’i’ vidi' is a comparative/relational phrase: 'a' here means 'in relation to' or 'compared to'; 'quel' = 'that/what' and 'ch’ i’ vidi' = 'that I saw' (ch’ = che, and vidi = I saw). The sequence functions as 'compared to that which I saw.'
segments.paradiso-33-g121: 'È tanto' is a simple copular clause: 'it is so much' or 'it is such (a thing).' 'Tanto' quantifies the enormity/intensity of what was seen.
segments.paradiso-33-g122: The result clause introduced by 'che' ('that') states the consequence: 'non basta a dicer 'poco'' = 'it does not suffice to say "little".' 'Non basta' = 'is not enough,' 'a dicer' is the archaic infinitive 'to say' (modern italiano 'a dire'), and 'poco' is quoted as the inadequate word.
segments.paradiso-33-g123: Invocation: 'O luce etterna' = 'O eternal light.' The relative clause 'che sola in te sidi' uses 'che' = 'who' and 'sola in te sidi' = 'who alone dwell/sit in thyself' (Dante's theological phrasing that God abides in himself). 'Sidi' is an older/poetic verbal form meaning 'sit/dwell' (not the modern 'siedi'), so this is an addressed attribute of the light.
segments.paradiso-33-g124: 'Sola t’intendi' repeats the 'alone' idea: literally 'alone you understand yourself' or 'alone are understood by yourself.' 'T’' is the clitic object/reflective pronoun 'you/yourself' and 'intendi' is present indicative 'you understand' (here reflexive in sense 'you understand/are intelligible to yourself').
segments.paradiso-33-g125: This coordinate phrase contains two participial qualities and the final verbs: 'e da te intelletta e intendente te' — 'da te intelletta' roughly 'being understood by yourself' (da te = by/from you; intelletta is a past participle) and 'intendente te' = 'and understanding you' (intend-ente = present participle). The final verbs 'ami e arridi' are present indicative, second person singular: 'you love and you smile' (arridi often 'smile upon'). Grammatically, the participles describe concurrent states (being understood and understanding) tied to the subject 'luce etterna' addressed above.

sentences.paradiso-33-s9: I beg you again, O queen—who can do what you will—that you keep his affections sound after so great a sight.
sentences.paradiso-33-s10: May your guard overcome human impulses:
sentences.paradiso-33-s12: God's beloved and venerated eyes, fixed on the speaker, showed how pleasing the devout prayers are to them; then they turned to the eternal light, in which one must not believe that so bright an eye is sent through any creature.
sentences.paradiso-33-s39: [ellipsis] End of Project Gutenberg's La Divina Commedia di Dante, by Dante Alighieri.
sentences.paradiso-33-s1: Virgin Mother, daughter of your Son, humble and exalted more than any creature, fixed term of eternal counsel, you are she who so ennobled human nature that its Maker did not disdain to make himself her creature.
sentences.paradiso-33-s2: In your womb love was rekindled; by whose warmth, in eternal peace, this flower has thus germinated.
sentences.paradiso-33-s3: Here you are to us the meridian torch of charity; and below, among mortals, you are a living fountain of hope.
sentences.paradiso-33-s4: Lady, you are so great and so powerful that whoever seeks grace and does not turn to you wants his longing to fly without wings.
sentences.paradiso-33-s21: O abundant grace by which I dared to thrust my face into the eternal light, so that there I wore out my sight!
sentences.paradiso-33-s22: In its depths I saw that, bound with love into one volume, what is spread through the universe: substances and accidents and their custom, as if fused together; and so that what I say is a simple light.
sentences.paradiso-33-s23: I believe I saw the universal form of this knot; for when I say this at greater length I feel that I enjoy it.
sentences.paradiso-33-s24: A single instant is, to me, a greater lethargy than twenty-five centuries were to the enterprise that made Neptune marvel at the shade of Argo.
sentences.paradiso-33-s5: Your kindness does not merely help those who ask; many times it freely anticipates the asking.
sentences.paradiso-33-s6: In you is mercy, in you pity, in you magnificence; in you is gathered whatever goodness there is in any creature.
sentences.paradiso-33-s7: Now this one, who from the lowest hollow of the universe up to here has seen the spiritual lives one by one, beseeches you, by grace, for so much power that he may, with his eyes, lift himself higher toward the ultimate salvation.
sentences.paradiso-33-s8: And I, who have never burned for my own sight more than I do for his, present all my petitions to you, and I pray that they not be few, so that by your prayers you may unbind every cloud from his mortality, so that the supreme delight may be unfolded to him.
sentences.paradiso-33-s13: And I, who was drawing near to the end of all desires, as I ought, ended the ardor of longing within me.
sentences.paradiso-33-s14: Bernard made a sign to me and smiled, so that I should look upward; but I was already, within myself, such as he wished: for my sight, coming pure, penetrated more and more through the ray of the lofty light that is true of itself.
sentences.paradiso-33-s15: From then on my sight was greater than words can show — which yields to such a vision — and memory yields to such an outrage.
sentences.paradiso-33-s16: As is he who sees while dreaming — after whose dream the impressed passion remains, and the rest does not return to the mind — such am I; for almost all my vision ceases, and still the sweetness that was born from it trickles into my heart.
sentences.paradiso-33-s25: Thus my mind, wholly suspended, gazed fixedly, motionless and attentive, and was always kindled with a desire to look.
sentences.paradiso-33-s26: Before that light one becomes such that it is impossible ever to consent to turn away from it to another aspect; for the good, which is the object of the will, is wholly received in it, and what is perfect there is defective outside it.
sentences.paradiso-33-s27: From now on my speech will be shorter, according to what I recall, than that of a child who still wets his tongue at the breast.
sentences.paradiso-33-s28: Not that in the living light I was looking at there was more than one simple appearance — for it is always as it was before — but because, by the vision that strengthened as I looked, a single appearance came into being; changing, it worked upon me.
sentences.paradiso-33-s17: Thus the snow is unsealed by the sun; thus, in the wind among the light leaves, the Sibyl’s utterance was carried away.
sentences.paradiso-33-s18: O highest Light, who so lifts yourself above mortal conceptions, restore to my mind a little of what you seemed, and make my tongue so powerful that a single spark of your glory may remain for future people;
sentences.paradiso-33-s19: I believe, because of the sharpness that I endured from the living ray, that I would have been lost, if my eyes had been turned away from him.
sentences.paradiso-33-s20: It reminds me that I was bolder for this to sustain it, so much that I reached my look with infinite power.
sentences.paradiso-33-s33: That circular motion, which, so conceived, seemed in you like reflected light, somewhat circumspect to my eyes, within itself, in its very colour, seemed to me painted with our likeness; wherefore my face was wholly set in it.
sentences.paradiso-33-s34: As the geometer who applies himself wholly to measuring the circle, and cannot find, thinking, that principle which he needs, such was I at that new vision: I wanted to see how the image fitted the circle and how it is set there; but my own feathers were not sufficient for that: except that my mind was struck by a brilliance in which its will/desire came.
sentences.paradiso-33-s35: To the lofty imagination here power failed; but already my desire and my will were turning, like a wheel that is moved equally, the love that moves the sun and the other stars.
sentences.paradiso-33-s36: TABLE OF SPECIAL CHARACTERS — à = a grave; è = e grave; ì = i grave; ò = o grave; ù = u grave; é = e acute; ó = o acute; ä = a umlaut; ë = e umlaut; ï = i umlaut; ö = o umlaut; ü = u umlaut; È = E grave; Ë = E umlaut; Ï = I umlaut; « = left angle quotation mark; » = right angle quotation mark; “ = left double quotation mark; ” = right double quotation mark; ‘ = left single quotation mark; ’ = right single quotation mark; — = em dash; • = middot.
sentences.paradiso-33-s29: Within the deep and clear subsistence of the lofty light there appeared to me three circles, of three colours and of one same circumference;
sentences.paradiso-33-s30: O how short is speech, and how faint it is compared with my conception!
sentences.paradiso-33-s31: And this, compared with what I saw, is so much that the word 'little' does not suffice to say it.
sentences.paradiso-33-s32: O eternal light, who alone dost sit in thyself, who alone understandest thyself, and, being understood by thyself and understanding thyself, thou lovest and thou dost smile!
